factual-accuracy.requirement: Explicitly states no published head-to-head DSM-5 vs ICD-11 diagnostic accuracy studies exist as of query date
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that “no direct head-to-head empirical comparison studies comparing DSM-5 and ICD-11 diagnostic accuracy, sensitivity, or specificity for ASD have been published” and reiterates “No direct head-to-head empirical comparison studies have been published as of May 2026,” matching the query date it provides.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States DSM-5 requires all 3 social-communication subdomains (social-emotional reciprocity, nonverbal behaviors, relationships)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that DSM-5 has three social-communication sub-criteria (A1, A2, A3) and that “all three must be present,” listing social-emotional reciprocity, nonverbal communication, and relationships as the three required subdomains.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States ICD-11 uses examples-based approach for social communication/reciprocity rather than fixed checklist
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response characterizes ICD-11 social-communication criteria as a “broad description with no fixed number of features required” and a “flexible approach,” contrasting it with DSM-5’s fixed sub-criteria checklist. This conveys an examples-based/non-checklist approach for social communication/reciprocity.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States DSM-5 requires at least 2 of 4 restricted and repetitive behavior categories and explicitly includes hyper- or hyporeactivity to sensory input as one category.
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that DSM-5 sensory processing is Criterion B4 under restricted/repetitive behaviors and explicitly quotes “Hyper- or hyporeactivity to sensory input…” as one of four B sub-criteria. It also explicitly says “only 2 of 4 B criteria must be met,” satisfying the “at least 2 of 4 categories” requirement.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States ICD-11 includes sensory reactivity within RRB domain as examples without numeric threshold
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that ICD-11 includes sensory processing as part of the restricted/repetitive behaviors domain, describing it as “atypical responses to sensory stimuli” and giving examples like sounds, textures, lights, pain, and temperature. It presents this as an example/manifestation rather than a numeric threshold requirement.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States DSM-5 uses three severity levels (Level 1, 2, 3) based on support needed
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes DSM-5 severity as “3 Levels of Support” and lists Level 1, Level 2, and Level 3 with descriptions framed around the amount of support required (e.g., “Requiring Support,” “Requiring Substantial Support,” “Requiring Very Substantial Support”). This satisfies stating DSM-5 uses three severity levels based on support needed.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States ICD-11 uses subcategories by intellectual development and language impairment (codes 6A02.0-6A02.5)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that ICD-11 autism severity profiles use subcategories based on intellectual development disorder and functional language impairment, and it lists codes 6A02.0 through 6A02.5 with corresponding descriptions.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States both require symptoms present in early developmental period but may manifest later when demands exceed capacities or be masked
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states DSM-5 requires symptoms present in the early developmental period and notes they may not become fully manifest until social demands exceed capacities. It also states ICD-11 onset occurs during the developmental period and that symptoms may manifest later when demands exceed capacities, and explicitly mentions masking/compensation.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States both require clinically significant impairment in social, occupational, or other important functioning areas
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states DSM-5 Criterion D requires “clinically significant impairment in social, occupational, or other important areas of current functioning” and that ICD-11 requires “significant impairment in personal, family, social, educational, occupational or other important areas of functioning,” indicating both manuals require clinically significant functional impairment.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Cites Kulage et al. 2019 (or similar 2019 meta-analysis) showing approximately 20-21% decrease in DSM-5 diagnoses vs DSM-IV-TR
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response cites a Kulage et al. meta-analysis and reports that about 20% of individuals diagnosed under DSM-IV-TR no longer met DSM-5 criteria, which corresponds to an approximately 20–21% decrease in DSM-5 diagnoses versus DSM-IV-TR.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Includes at least 1 systematic review or meta-analysis with study design, sample size, and pooled statistics specified
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response includes a systematic review and meta-analysis (Kulage et al., 2020) and specifies the study design, sample size (33 studies), and pooled quantitative findings (e.g., ~20% no longer meeting DSM-5 criteria). This satisfies the requirement for at least one systematic review/meta-analysis with design, sample size, and pooled statistics.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Includes at least 1 primary empirical study (e.g., Wiggins et al. 2019 SEED2 n=1,061 or similar) with sample size and sensitivity/specificity data
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response does not include any primary empirical study directly comparing DSM-5 vs ICD-11 with reported sample size and sensitivity/specificity. The only sensitivity/specificity figures provided (e.g., McPartland et al. 2012; Grapel et al. 2015) are not DSM-5 vs ICD-11 comparisons and one is outside the 2013–present window.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Reports GRADE quality ratings (High/Moderate/Low/Very Low) for at least 2 sources with justification
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response assigns GRADE ratings with brief justifications for multiple sources (e.g., Kamp-Becker 2024 rated Very Low due to being a perspective article; First et al. 2021 rated Moderate due to structured methodology; McPartland 2012 and Kulage 2020 rated Moderate with design-based rationale). This satisfies reporting GRADE quality ratings for at least two sources with justification.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes available evidence compares DSM-5 vs DSM-IV or discusses ICD-11 conceptually rather than head-to-head testing
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly notes that no direct head-to-head DSM-5 vs ICD-11 diagnostic accuracy studies exist and instead synthesizes indirect evidence (e.g., DSM-5 vs DSM-IV comparisons) and conceptual/system-level ICD-11 vs DSM-5 discussions (e.g., First et al., Kamp-Becker). This matches the criterion’s requirement to acknowledge the evidence base is largely DSM-5 vs DSM-IV or conceptual ICD-11 comparisons.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States WHO released ICD-11 CDDR in March 2024 with ASD code 6A02
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response mentions ICD-11 and repeatedly references ASD code 6A02, but it does not state that WHO released the ICD-11 CDDR in March 2024. There is no March 2024 CDDR release date claim anywhere in the text, so the required combined statement is missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States WHO ICD-11 includes atypical sensory reactivity within restricted/inflexible RRB domain
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that ICD-11 includes “atypical responses to sensory stimuli” under the restricted/repetitive behaviors domain (e.g., in Section A.2 and again in Section C.1), indicating it is part of the restricted/inflexible RRB domain.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States APA DSM-5 requires all 3 social-communication subdomains and at least 2 of 4 RRBs
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that DSM-5 requires deficits in all three social-communication criteria and that at least two of the four restricted/repetitive behavior criteria must be met (noting sensory issues are B4 within the four).

factual-accuracy.requirement: States AAP 2020 clinical report references DSM-5 for US practice
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses the AAP 2020 clinical report using DSM-5 as the reference framework, but it does not state that the AAP 2020 clinical report references DSM-5 specifically for US practice. There is no explicit linkage made between the AAP’s DSM-5 use and its role as the US practice standard.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States AAP notes limited/inconclusive evidence for standalone sensory processing disorder diagnosis
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response notes that the AAP advised against diagnosing sensory processing disorder as a standalone condition, but it does not state that the AAP considers the evidence for standalone SPD diagnosis limited or inconclusive. It also does not explicitly use “limited/inconclusive evidence” language or an equivalent formulation about the evidence base.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States NICE or NHS England 2024 framework specifies ICD-11 criteria for primary clinical description
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses NICE CG128 and NHS England ICD-11 implementation, but it does not state that any NICE or NHS England 2024 framework specifies ICD-11 criteria for a “primary clinical description.” No 2024 NICE/NHS England framework is cited or described with that specific requirement.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States NHS requires ICD-10 mapping for national datasets during transition period
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses NHS use of ICD-10 and ongoing ICD-11 implementation, but it does not state that the NHS requires ICD-10 mapping for national datasets during the transition period. No explicit requirement about ICD-10-to-ICD-11 mapping for national datasets is mentioned.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States ASAN supported inclusive unified spectrum diagnosis accounting for masking and compensation
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes ASAN’s advocacy around DSM-5 and explicitly notes ASAN highlighted “compensation and masking behaviors” leading to underdiagnosis, implying support for a unified spectrum diagnosis that accounts for masking/compensation.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States National Autistic Society presents both DSM-5 and ICD-11 as largely similar regarding core characteristics
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response says the National Autistic Society notes diagnosis can be based on DSM-5 or ICD-11 and treats both as standard tools, but it does not state that NAS presents them as largely similar regarding core characteristics. It even says NAS has “no explicit comparative position,” which fails to assert “largely similar.”

factual-accuracy.requirement: States NAS uses identity-affirming or neurodiversity-affirming language
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes the National Autistic Society’s stance using affirming framing (e.g., referencing the “neurodiversity paradigm,” “neurodivergence,” and noting NICE editorial updates to use “autistic people” language), indicating NAS uses identity-affirming/neurodiversity-affirming language.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Autism Speaks provides DSM-5 text but does not take formal position on DSM vs ICD preference
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses Autism Speaks’ DSM-5 criteria page and notes it has no specific ICD-11 position statement, but it does not state that Autism Speaks provides DSM-5 text while taking no formal position on DSM vs ICD preference. There is no explicit sentence clarifying neutrality on DSM vs ICD preference by Autism Speaks.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States meta-analysis or empirical evidence shows DSM-5 resulted in approximately 20-21% decrease in diagnoses vs DSM-IV-TR
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response cites a meta-analysis (Kulage et al., 2020) stating that DSM-5 led to an approximately 20% reduction in ASD diagnoses compared with DSM-IV-TR. It does not state an approximately 20–21% decrease specifically framed as “DSM-5 resulted in ~20–21% decrease in diagnoses vs DSM-IV-TR,” nor does it provide the 21% figure.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes DSM-5 showed reduced sensitivity for PDD-NOS, Asperger profiles, or cognitively able subgroups
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response reports subgroup sensitivity drops under DSM-5 (e.g., Asperger’s 25%, PDD-NOS 28%, and lower sensitivity for IQ ≥70) and explicitly notes DSM-5 under-identifies higher-functioning individuals and those previously diagnosed with PDD-NOS/Asperger’s. This directly reflects reduced sensitivity for those profiles/subgroups.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes ICD-11 breadth and compensation language may increase false positives or over-diagnosis (concern awaiting empirical testing)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly warns that ICD-11’s broader, less prescriptive criteria may increase heterogeneity and create a “large danger of false positive diagnoses,” potentially increasing prevalence and diluting services, and it frames this as a concern raised in conceptual critique rather than empirically confirmed. It also notes the lack of direct head-to-head empirical comparisons, implying the concern awaits empirical testing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States sensory features have high sensitivity but lower specificity for ASD (occur in other neurodevelopmental conditions)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that sensory issues can show high sensitivity but poor specificity for ASD, citing Grapel et al. (2015) with an example of sensitivity >0.75 and specificity 0.34, and explicitly notes sensory features are frequent in other disorders.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States US uses ICD-10-CM code F84.0 for autism spectrum disorder
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that the United States uses ICD-10-CM and identifies the primary autism billing code as F84.0 ("Autistic disorder"), describing it as the primary autism code used for billing and reimbursement.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States US has no official ICD-11-CM implementation date or transition timeline announced
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that the US has not adopted ICD-11 for billing and gives projected transition timelines (e.g., 2027–2029), but it does not explicitly say that the US has no official ICD-11-CM implementation date or transition timeline announced. It also implies planning via an NCVHS workgroup, which is not the same as stating no official date/timeline exists.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States NHS England uses ICD-11 for clinical description of autism, noting that the transition from ICD-10 to ICD-11 is underway.
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that the UK NHS still primarily uses ICD-10 and that ICD-11 implementation is underway but incomplete, and it discusses NHS England’s work toward ICD-11 rollout. This satisfies the requirement to note the transition from ICD-10 to ICD-11 is underway and that NHS England uses ICD-11 for clinical description in that context.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States UK still requires ICD-10 mapping for national datasets during transition
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response says the UK “still primarily uses ICD-10 within the NHS” and that ICD-11 implementation is underway/piloting, which implies ICD-10 mapping remains necessary for national datasets during the transition.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States WHO ICD-11 took effect January 1, 2022 for member states
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that ICD-11 “came into effect on January 1, 2022” and frames this as WHO ICD-11 taking effect for member states.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explicitly flags absence of direct DSM-5 vs ICD-11 studies and distinguishes projections/inferences from empirical data
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly states that “no direct head-to-head empirical comparison studies” of DSM-5 vs ICD-11 diagnostic accuracy have been published and labels the remaining evidence as indirect/conceptual. It repeatedly distinguishes conceptual critiques and DSM-5-vs-DSM-IV meta-analytic findings from direct DSM-5-vs-ICD-11 empirical data, framing conclusions about ICD-11 as projections/inferences rather than measured outcomes.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Quantifies diagnostic impact by subgroup (e.g., ~21% decrease overall, differential PDD-NOS/Asperger/cognitively able outcomes)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response quantifies diagnostic impact, citing a ~20% reduction in ASD diagnoses under DSM-5 (Kulage meta-analysis) and reporting subgroup sensitivity differences for Autistic Disorder (76%), Asperger’s (25%), PDD-NOS (28%), and IQ ≥70 vs <70 (46% vs 70%). These figures address overall and differential subgroup outcomes.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Contrasts DSM-5 numeric RRB threshold (≥2 of 4) vs ICD-11 clinical judgment approach without fixed count
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response contrasts DSM-5’s fixed numeric threshold for restricted/repetitive behaviors (at least 2 of 4 B criteria, with sensory as B4) with ICD-11’s non-count-based, clinician-judgment approach (described as having no fixed number of features required and flexible symptom documentation).

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Distinguishes test accuracy studies from clinical utility perspectives and provides GRADE ratings with justification
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response separates evidence about diagnostic accuracy (e.g., sensitivity/specificity figures and the meta-analysis) from broader clinical utility and conceptual critiques, and it assigns GRADE ratings (e.g., Very Low/Low/Moderate) with brief justifications tied to study design and directness.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Integrates sensory role, functional impairment, and social-communication + RRB dyad into actionable clinical decision guidance
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response links sensory processing to the RRB domain and discusses functional impairment thresholds and social-communication requirements, then translates these differences into clinical decision guidance (e.g., under- vs over-diagnosis risks, sensory-dominant presentation risk table, and differential-diagnosis cautions). It explicitly frames actionable implications for clinicians based on the social-communication + RRB dyad plus impairment and sensory weighting.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explains disconnect between clinical ICD-11 adoption and administrative ICD-10 mapping/coding during transition
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly describes a transition gap where clinicians use DSM-5 criteria while billing remains on ICD-10-CM (e.g., F84.0), noting the US has not adopted ICD-11 for billing and discussing mapping/training/implementation timelines and costs. This directly explains the disconnect between clinical ICD-11 adoption concepts and administrative ICD-10 mapping/coding during transition.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies ICD-11 breadth trading sensitivity for specificity as hypothesis requiring empirical validation rather than settled fact
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response repeatedly characterizes ICD-11 as broader/more permissive and links that to potential overdiagnosis, but it presents these claims as conclusions (e.g., “primarily risks overdiagnosis,” “304 possible presentations”) rather than explicitly framing the breadth–sensitivity/specificity tradeoff as a hypothesis needing empirical validation. It notes an evidence gap (“no direct head-to-head empirical comparison studies”) but does not clearly label the tradeoff itself as unvalidated.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: States DSM-5 and ICD-11 are essentially identical or interchangeable without acknowledging differences in RRB thresholds or severity systems
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response does not claim DSM-5 and ICD-11 are interchangeable without differences; it repeatedly emphasizes meaningful differences (e.g., DSM-5 requiring all 3 social-communication criteria vs ICD-11 flexibility, and different severity systems). Although it quotes a source saying some disorders are “essentially identical,” it does not apply that to autism and explicitly notes ASD has major differences.

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes response with six lettered sections (A through F) addressing each query component as specified
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is organized into six clearly labeled sections: Section A, Section B, Section C, Section D, Section E, and Section F, corresponding to the query’s A–F components. Each section addresses the respective component (table, evidence synthesis, guidelines, advocacy, safety risks, and coding/reimbursement).

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents DSM-5 vs ICD-11 criteria comparison in a table or structured side-by-side format for Section A
presentation-quality.explanation: Section A includes multiple side-by-side tables comparing DSM-5 and ICD-11, including a structured comparison for core social-communication criteria and other required rows.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses precise diagnostic and psychometric terminology (e.g., sensitivity, specificity, GRADE, diagnostic criteria)
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses diagnostic/psychometric terminology such as “sensitivity,” “specificity,” “false positives/false negatives,” “diagnostic accuracy,” “systematic review and meta-analysis,” and assigns “GRADE quality ratings” to sources.

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains scholarly, evidence-based tone distinguishing empirical findings from clinical inference or opinion
presentation-quality.explanation: The response generally uses a formal, academic tone, cites sources, and repeatedly flags evidence gaps (e.g., noting no head-to-head DSM-5 vs ICD-11 accuracy studies) while labeling some claims as “inferred” rather than explicit positions. It distinguishes conceptual critiques/perspective pieces from empirical studies and assigns GRADE ratings accordingly.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents DSM-5 vs ICD-11 criteria comparison as prose paragraphs without table or structured side-by-side format
presentation-quality.explanation: The response does not present the DSM-5 vs ICD-11 comparison purely as prose paragraphs. It includes multiple side-by-side tables (e.g., in Section A for core criteria, sensory processing, severity, onset, and impairment), which is a structured format rather than unstructured prose.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses colloquial phrasing (e.g., 'sensory stuff', 'pretty similar', 'basically the same') instead of clinical terminology
presentation-quality.explanation: The response consistently uses formal clinical terminology (e.g., “restricted, repetitive behaviors,” “hyper- or hyporeactivity,” “sensitivity/specificity,” “functional impairment”) and does not use colloquial phrases like “sensory stuff,” “pretty similar,” or “basically the same.”

citation-quality.requirement: Cites official DSM-5 sources from APA, CDC, or cdc.gov/ncbddd/autism/hcp-dsm.html
citation-quality.explanation: The response does not cite any official DSM-5 source from the APA, CDC, or the CDC DSM-5 autism criteria page (cdc.gov/ncbddd/autism/hcp-dsm.html). It references non-official summaries (e.g., Autism Speaks, The Transmitter) and other sites, but no APA DSM-5 page or CDC DSM-5 criteria page is included.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites official ICD-11 sources from WHO CDDR (who.int) or pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov for ICD-11 clinical descriptions
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites official WHO ICD-11 material from who.int (e.g., the WHO ICD-11 implementation FAQ and ICD-11 release pages) and also includes multiple ICD-11-related clinical description sources hosted on pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov (e.g., the ICD-11 innovations article). These satisfy the requirement to cite official ICD-11 sources from WHO CDDR (who.int) or PMC for ICD-11 clinical descriptions.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites peer-reviewed medical/psychiatric journals via PubMed (pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov), PMC, BMC Medicine, Molecular Psychiatry, or similar for evidence synthesis
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites multiple peer-reviewed journal articles via PMC and PubMed, including World Psychiatry (PMC link), McPartland et al. (PMC link), Grapel et al. (PMC link), and Doernberg & Hollander (PubMed link). These sources are used in the evidence synthesis section and the references list.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites WHO ICD-11 implementation materials or clinical guidance from who.int
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites WHO ICD-11 implementation materials hosted on who.int, including “ICD-11 Implementation — World Health Organization” (https://www.who.int/standards/classifications/frequently-asked-questions/icd-11-implementation) and a WHO ICD-11 release news item (https://www.who.int/news/item/16-02-2026-icd-11-2026-release).

citation-quality.requirement: Cites at least 2 of NICE (nice.org.uk), AAP (publications.aap.org), NHS England (england.nhs.uk) for organizational positions
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites NICE via multiple nice.org.uk links (e.g., NICE CG128) but does not cite AAP from publications.aap.org or NHS England from england.nhs.uk (the NHS England link provided is portal6.phin.org.uk). Therefore it does not cite at least two of the specified domains.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites at least 2 of ASAN (autisticadvocacy.org), National Autistic Society (autism.org.uk), Autism Speaks for advocacy perspectives
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites ASAN via an ASAN policy brief on autisticadvocacy.org and also cites the National Autistic Society via multiple autism.org.uk pages, and it additionally cites Autism Speaks pages.

citation-quality.requirement: For evidence synthesis sources (systematic reviews, meta-analyses, primary studies), uses publications dated 2013 or later per query requirement
citation-quality.explanation: The response’s evidence synthesis includes a key source from 2012 (McPartland, Reichow & Volkmar, 2012), which is earlier than the required 2013–present window. Although it also cites several 2013+ sources (e.g., 2016, 2020, 2021, 2024), the inclusion of a pre-2013 study means the criterion is not satisfied.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Reddit, patient forums, WebMD comments, or social media posts as sources for diagnostic criteria or clinical evidence
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites sources like The Transmitter, Wikipedia, and various blogs/clinic sites, but it does not cite Reddit, patient forums, WebMD comments, or social media posts as evidence for diagnostic criteria or clinical claims.
